title: Relevant Precedents for SignWriting Infrastructure
subtitle: What comparable archive, language, data, and research-infrastructure models suggest
summary: SignWriting follows a recognizable infrastructure path: mature assets first, institutional anchoring second, staffing and continuity next, and grants or philanthropy after that.
synopsis: Sutton SignWriting is unusual, but the institutional pattern it needs is not. Comparable archive, data, language, sign-language, and research-infrastructure models include ICPSR, ELDP/ELAR, PARADISEC, BSL Corpus and SignBank, ASLLRP, and MIDAS.

slides.title: Why Precedents Matter
slides.summary: SignWriting is unusual, but the infrastructure pattern it needs is recognizable.

image_prompts.concrete: Precedent slide showing SignWriting beside archive, language, data, sign-language corpus, and research-infrastructure examples
image_prompts.abstract: An unusual asset following a recognizable infrastructure path

bullets: Funding becomes more credible after structure
slides.speech: The point of precedents is to show that SignWriting is unusual, but the infrastructure pattern it needs is not. Mature assets often come first. Anchoring, staffing, and funding structures follow when institutions recognize the value already present.
slides.notes: This slide prevents the case from sounding sui generis.

slides.title: Closest Analogs
slides.summary: Archive, language documentation, corpus, and data-infrastructure examples show how existing assets become durable programs.

image_prompts.concrete: Comparison slide with ICPSR, ELDP/ELAR, PARADISEC, BSL Corpus and SignBank, ASLLRP, and MIDAS as precedent models
image_prompts.abstract: Several infrastructure precedents mapped to shared stewardship patterns

bullets: ICPSR and MIDAS for data infrastructure
ELDP, ELAR, and PARADISEC for language archives
BSL Corpus and SignBank for sign-language resources
ASLLRP and ASL resources for adjacent models
slides.speech: The precedents are not identical, but they are useful. Data infrastructure, language archives, sign-language corpora, sign banks, and research platforms all show ways that valuable assets become more durable through institutional structure.
slides.notes: Use analogs carefully, not as exact matches.

slides.title: Shared Pattern
slides.summary: The shared pattern is mature assets first, institutional anchoring second, staffing and credibility next, then grants, philanthropy, and growth.

image_prompts.concrete: Pattern slide showing four stages: mature assets, institutional anchoring, staffing and credibility, grants philanthropy and growth
image_prompts.abstract: A staged infrastructure path from assets to durable growth

bullets: Grants, philanthropy, and growth after that
slides.speech: Across the examples, the pattern is recognizable: mature assets first, institutional anchoring second, staffing and credibility next, and then grants, philanthropy, and longer-term growth become more plausible.

slides.title: How To Use Precedents
slides.summary: Precedents should support host conversations without implying that any one model can simply be copied.

image_prompts.concrete: Institutional strategy slide showing precedents informing but not dictating a SignWriting host structure
image_prompts.abstract: Several precedent paths feeding into one tailored stewardship model

bullets: Use precedents to make the pattern legible
slides.speech: The precedents should make the pattern legible. They should not be copied mechanically. The useful question is which functions SignWriting needs and which host, consortium, or fiscal-sponsor model can provide them responsibly.
